segments.s016-g17: جهاندار is the subject meaning 'the sovereign' (here used as the agent). پیش + جهان‌آفرین is a prepositional phrase placed after the subject in classical style: پیش means 'in the presence of / before' and جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' (the Creator). Word order is Topic (جهاندار) + prepositional phrase indicating location/face-to-face (پیش جهان‌آفرین).
segments.s016-g18: نیایش همی کرد is an older/poetic sequence where همی gives an ongoing/habitual aspect ('was offering / did offer'); کرد is the light verb 'to do' with نیایش as its object (to perform a prayer). و links to the next verb clause; خواند آفرین here means 'he uttered/recited/shouted “Afarin”' (خواند = recite/utter, آفرین is an acclamation).
segments.s016-g19: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for' (referring back to the previous action). او را is the object marked by را ('him' as the recipient). فروغی functions as a noun 'a radiance' (lit. 'radiant [thing]'); چنین هدیه داد is 'gave such a gift' (داد = gave). Word order is typical classical Persian: relative particle + object/subject + object marker + noun phrase + verb.
segments.s016-g20: همین آتش 'this same fire' is the topic (the very fire just mentioned). آنگاه is an adverb 'then/thereafter'. قبله نهاد is a semi-fixed expression where نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed' — 'set/made (it) the qibla' (قبله = qibla / object of devotion). The verb follows the adverbial phrase, consistent with Persian flexible order.
segments.s016-g21: بگفتا is a past/hearsay verb 'he said' (archaic/epic form). فروغی است این ایزدی is a clause inverted for emphasis: فروغی (the radiance) + است (is) + این ایزدی ('this is divine' — ایزدی = 'divine/sacred'). The adjective ایزدی follows the demonstrative, a normal classical order for predicates.
segments.s016-g22: پرستید باید is a classical ordering where the verbal idea 'to worship' appears before the modal باید ('it is necessary / must'), so the sense is 'one must worship.' اگر بخردی is a conditional 'if (you) are wise' where بخردی here functions idiomatically as 'having wisdom / being wise.' In modern Persian the order would be اگر بخردی باید پرستی or باید آن را پرستید; classical verse permits this inversion.
segments.s016-g23: شب آمد is a simple past clause 'night came.' بر افروخت آتش is a causative/perfective verbal phrase 'he kindled/ignited a fire' where بر can be read as a particle prefixed to the verb (classical usage) and آتش is the direct object. چو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain' modifying آتش, so the fire is described as rising 'like a mountain.'
segments.s016-g24: همان شاه is the topical subject 'that very king.' در گرد او is a prepositional phrase 'around him' (در گرد = around/about), and با گروه 'with a group/company' indicates accompaniment. The phrase describes the king's position and those attending him.
segments.s016-g25: یکی here is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' (not the numeral). جشن کرد is a light-verb construction: noun جشن + past verb کرد = 'held a celebration'. آن شب is a temporal phrase 'that night' placed after the verb, and و connects the coordinated clause باده خورد, where خورد is the past tense of خوردن used with باده to mean 'drank (wine).'
segments.s016-g26: نام ... کرد is another light-verb construction meaning 'to name/call'; سده is fronted as the name assigned ('Sadeh'), آن جشن is the thing being named, and فرخنده is an adjectival complement meaning 'auspicious' — all governed by the past verb کرد 'he made/called'.
segments.s016-g27: ز introduces origin/association ('from' or 'of' Hushang). ماند is the past tense 'remained/was left', and the remainder این سده یادگار states what remained — 'this Sadeh (is) a memorial'. Classical word order places the verb early and the noun phrase describing what remained after it.
segments.s016-g28: بسی باد is an optative formula: بسی (much/many) + باد (classical optative 'may it be') = 'may there be many'. چون او modifies دگر شهریار: 'kings like him'. The whole clause expresses a wish ('May there be many rulers like him').
segments.s016-g29: کز is a relative/causal particle here ('who/for that'), introducing the reason clause. آباد کردن جهان is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase 'making the world prosperous', and شاد کرد is the main past clause 'made (it) happy' with کرد as the past-tense light verb; the object ('it' = جهان) is understood from context.
segments.s016-g30: جهانی functions as 'the whole world' or 'people everywhere' as the subject. به نیکی is an adverbial phrase of manner ('in goodness/in praise'), where به marks manner; از او introduces the person being remembered ('of him'); یاد کرد is the compound 'to remember/mention' formed by noun یاد + light verb کرد in the past tense.
segments.s016-g1: This is a compact nominal and directional phrase with narrative 'one day' first. 'یکی روز' is a poetic indefinite/narrative 'one (a certain) day'; 'شاه جهان' is a title (noun + genitive-like epithet) meaning 'king of the world'; 'سوی کوه' is a prepositional phrase 'toward the mountain' giving direction. Word order (indefinite time → subject/title → direction) is literary but straightforward.
segments.s016-g2: 'گذر کرد' is a past tense verbal phrase 'passed' (کرد as past auxiliary/lexical verb after the noun 'گذر'); the following prepositional phrase 'با چند کس هم‌گروه' means 'with several people of the same group' — 'چند' here = 'a few/several' and 'هم‌گروه' qualifies those people. The verb precedes the 'with'-phrase as is normal in Persian.
segments.s016-g3: 'پدید آمد' is a past tense compound meaning 'appeared' (پدید + آمد). 'از دور' is an adverbial 'from afar'; 'چیزی دراز' is the indefinite object 'a long thing.' The word order places the verb before the adverbial and object for poetic effect, but grammatically it's Verb + source + object.
segments.s016-g4: This is a string of adjectival/descriptive compounds linked by 'و'. 'سیه رنگ' = 'black-colored', 'تیره تن' = 'dark-bodied' (adjective + noun), and 'تیز تاز' are two coordinated adjectives meaning 'swift' and 'fierce' (an intensifying pair). The sequence describes the appearance of the thing just mentioned.
segments.s016-g5: 'دو چشم' is the subject 'two eyes'. The small phrase 'از بر سر' is an archaic/poetic locative meaning 'on (the) head' (a fixed classical combination); 'چو دو چشمه خون' is a simile introduced by 'چو' = 'like', 'two springs of blood'. The structure is subject + locative + simile.
segments.s016-g6: 'ز' is the poetic variant of 'از' = 'from'; 'دود دهانش' = 'the smoke of its mouth' where '-ش' on 'دهان' is the enclitic pronoun 'its/his'. 'جهان تیره‌گون' is the result clause 'the world (became) dark-hued' with the verb ('became/made') omitted in terse poetic style: the smoke from its mouth darkened the world. 'تیره‌گون' is an adjectival compound meaning 'dark-colored/gloomy'.
segments.s016-g7: 'نگه کرد' (noun 'نگه' + کرد) functions as 'kept watch' or 'gazed'; the proper name with epithet 'هوشنگ باهوش' follows the verb in inverted literary order ('Hushang the Wise'). The conjunction 'و' links this action to what follows, and the bare noun 'سنگ' at the end begins the next action (object 'a stone').
segments.s016-g8: 'گرفتش' is a past-tense verb 'he seized it' (گرفت + enclitic object -ش referring back to the creature); 'یکی سنگ' = 'one stone' (numeric 'one' + noun) indicates a single stone involved. 'و شد' = 'and became', and 'تیز چنگ' is the predicate 'a sharp claw' (adjective + noun). In this line the verb 'شد' links the preceding noun phrase to the resulting state; classical poetry often packs several actions together, so the referent of '-ش' and which noun becomes 'تیز چنگ' is left compact and somewhat ambiguous but typically read as 'he seized it; a single stone (used) and it became a sharp claw.'
segments.s016-g9: به زور کیانی is a prepositional adverbial phrase meaning 'by/with Kayanian force' (به + زور + adjective/possessive). رهانید is the past tense form of رهانیدن ('released/freed') and here carries the main action; the direct object دست follows the verb in this poetic order (verb before object).
segments.s016-g10: جهان‌سوز مار is a compound noun phrase 'the world‑destroying serpent' with the descriptive adjective قبل از اسم (جهان‌سوز before مار). از introduces the source/point the serpent sprang from, and جست is the past of جستن meaning 'leapt/sprung' (third‑person past).
segments.s016-g11: بر آمد is the compound verb 'برآمد' meaning 'rose up/appeared'. The phrase به سنگ گران‌سنگ خرد is a prepositional phrase ('onto/against a heavy/precious small stone') where modifiers (گران‌سنگ, خرد) follow the head noun in poetic order; به marks direction/target.
segments.s016-g12: همان و همین (a double emphatic) marks 'that very' or 'the very same'; سنگ is the noun subject and بشکست is the past passive/ruptive 'was broken/shattered'. گرد after بشکست functions as a resultant particle 'into dust/into pieces' (classical/poetic usage).
segments.s016-g13: فروغی پدید آمد is 'a radiance appeared' — فروغی (indefinite noun) + پدید آمد (p.p. verb 'appeared/was manifested'). از هر دو سنگ marks the source 'from both stones' (از = from, هر دو = both).
segments.s016-g14: دل سنگ گشت is a copular clause: دل (heart) + سنگ گشت (گشت = 'became/turned', with سنگ as the predicate complement 'stone'). از فروغ آذرنگ gives the cause/source ('from the fiery radiance'); آذرنگ here is adjectival 'fiery‑colored'.
segments.s016-g15: نشد مار کشته is an impersonal/negative perfect construction: نشد ('did not happen' / 'was not') + مار (object/subject noun) + کشته (past participle 'killed') → 'the snake was not killed.' ولیکن ز راز introduces a contrast 'but/however, because of/by a secret'.
segments.s016-g16: از این طبع سنگ is a complex prepositional phrase 'from this stony temperament/nature' (از = from; طبع سنگ = 'stony nature'). آتش آمد فراز = 'fire came up' with فراز as an adverbial 'up/on high' indicating upward emergence; آمد is the past 'came/rose'.

sentences.s016-s9: The sovereign stood before the Creator, offered prayers and cried out “Afarin.”
sentences.s016-s10: For he gave him such a radiance as a gift; that very fire he then set as the qibla.
sentences.s016-s11: He said, 'This radiance is divine; if you are wise, you must worship it.'
sentences.s016-s12: Night came; he kindled a fire that rose like a mountain — that same king with a company around him.
sentences.s016-s13: Someone held a feast that night and drank wine; he named that festival Sadeh and made it auspicious.
sentences.s016-s14: This Sadeh remained as a memorial of Hushang; may there be many kings like him.
sentences.s016-s15: Because he made the world prosperous, he made it joyful; the whole world remembered him for his goodness.
sentences.s016-s1: One day the king of the world passed toward the mountain with a few companions.
sentences.s016-s2: From afar there appeared a long something, black in color, dark-bodied, and swift and fierce.
sentences.s016-s3: Two eyes upon its head like two springs of blood; from its mouth smoke made the world dark-hued.
sentences.s016-s4: Hushang the Wise looked (watched) and picked up a stone; he seized one stone, and it became a sharp claw.
sentences.s016-s5: By Kayanian might he freed his hand; the world‑destroying serpent leapt from the world‑seeker.
sentences.s016-s6: A heavy, precious little stone rose up against (him); that very stone was shattered to dust.
sentences.s016-s7: A radiance appeared from both stones; the heart turned to stone from the fiery radiance.
sentences.s016-s8: The snake was not killed; yet, by a secret — from this stony nature — fire rose up.
